nativeRange.habitat: Small, slow-moving streams, minor tributaries, and flooded forests with overhanging vegetation and leaf litter; tea-coloured, soft, acidic blackwater typical of the lower Amazon basin and coastal Guiana drainages. Fish congregate at or near the surface beneath emergent leaves.

decorPreferences: floating plants (frogbit, Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to provide surface cover and spawning triggers
tall stem plants that grow out over the waterline as egg-laying sites
driftwood branches positioned to emerge from water surface
dried leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) to darken water and release tannins
dim lighting to replicate shaded stream canopy
tight-fitting lid — this species is an accomplished jumper and will use any gap

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on surface insects (ants, Ephemeroptera larvae), small invertebrates, and worms. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality micro-pellets and fine flake as a staple, but thrives with daily or near-daily offerings of small live or frozen foods: Artemia nauplii, Moina, Daphnia, micro-bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, cyclops, and small Drosophila or other fruit flies presented at the surface. Surface feeding is strongly preferred — food should be light enough to float or sink slowly.

goodWith: small characids of similar size (e.g., ember tetras, cardinal tetras)
small Corydoras and dwarf Corydoras catfish
freshwater dwarf shrimp (with caution — fry may be predated)
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that outcompete for surface food

summary: The Splash Tetra has one of the most extraordinary reproductive strategies in freshwater fishkeeping: pairs leap from the water together to deposit eggs on overhanging leaves, then the male splashes them every ~30 min to keep them moist until hatching ~3 days later. Peaceful surface-dweller from Amazon blackwater; reaches ~6 cm. Needs a tight lid and floating cover.
careNotes:
The Splash Tetra requires a tank of at least 75 L with a footprint of 90 × 30 cm or larger — footprint matters more than depth, since this is a surface-dwelling species. A secure, tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: Copella arnoldi is an accomplished jumper, and individuals will escape through any opening, even during the normal course of daily activity and certainly during spawning leaps. Floating plants such as frogbit or water lettuce serve a dual purpose: they provide surface cover that reduces skittishness, and they present potential spawning sites. Tall stems or driftwood branches that emerge above the waterline are ideal spawning triggers.

Water should be soft and acidic to neutral (pH 4.0–7.5, ideally 6.0–7.0 for long-term keeping; gH 1–12, kH 0–6; temperature 20–28°C, ideally 24–27°C). Although the species can tolerate a wider pH range in captivity, blackwater conditions — supplemented with Indian almond leaves or peat filtration — more closely replicate their native habitat, enhance coloration, and are associated with improved spawning willingness. Dim lighting suits them well.

Shoaling is important: maintain a group of at least 8–10 individuals with mixed sexes. A smaller group will be shy, stress easily, and rarely spawn. Males are distinguished by larger size, a red spot behind the eye, and a more extended elongated upper caudal lobe. Females are smaller and plumper when in condition. Feed a varied diet heavy in small live and frozen invertebrates offered at or near the surface; food that sinks quickly may be ignored.

The species is broadly compatible with other soft-water, blackwater community fish of similar or smaller size. Avoid boisterous species that monopolise surface space or food, and avoid fin-nippers. The Splash Tetra is not well-suited to a general hard-water community tank. Gradual acclimatisation to new water is advisable given sensitivity to pH swings.
breedingNotes:
The reproductive strategy of Copella arnoldi is unique among aquarium fish and is the species’ most famous attribute. When conditioned with plentiful live food and presented with suitable overhanging spawning sites (emergent plant leaves, a slate or acrylic panel placed just above the waterline, or leaves of plants like Amazon sword or Echinodorus grown out over the rim), males will court females and the pair will make repeated synchronised leaps of up to 5–7 cm above the water surface, clinging together to a leaf or other surface and depositing a small clutch of 5–10 adhesive eggs per leap. Multiple leaps build up a clutch of 80–200 eggs at a single site.

After spawning, the male takes up station beneath the egg mass and uses powerful flicks of his caudal fin to splash water upward onto the eggs at intervals of approximately 30 minutes. This behaviour is sustained continuously for 36–72 hours (typically ~3 days) until hatching. If the eggs dry out, they will die, so the male’s splashing is essential. The female takes no part in this parental care. Water level in the breeding tank should be set so that eggs deposited ~2–4 cm above the surface can be reached by the male’s splashing.

Eggs hatch after approximately 60–72 hours; newly hatched fry fall or wriggle into the water below. Once the yolk sac is absorbed (after ~2 further days), fry are free-swimming and accept infusoria, paramecia, or commercially prepared first-foods, progressing quickly to Artemia nauplii and micro-worms. The breeding tank should have a very gentle filter (sponge filter) to avoid sucking up fry. Remove the male once the eggs have hatched. Breeding in captivity is considered straightforward once the fish are properly conditioned and a suitable spawning site is provided, though the unusual requirements (emergent leaf or surface, correct water level, tight lid) mean most beginner setups are not configured for it by default.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Copella arnoldi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (45–55 mm SL), temperature 20–28°C, pH 4.0–7.5, hardness 18–143 ppm (approx. 1–12 dH), native range (lower Amazon basin, coastal Guiana drainages), habitat (streams, flooded forest, overhanging vegetation, blackwater), diet (micropredator, live/frozen invertebrates), tank footprint (90 × 30 cm minimum), grouping (8–10+), compatible tankmates, and breeding behaviour (pair leaps, male splashing 36–72 h until hatch).

additional.name: FishBase — Copella arnoldi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult TL (4.2 cm male), temperature (25–29°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (dH 5–12), native range (lower Rio Amazonas, Pará, Amapá, Guyana, French Guiana, Suriname, mouth of Rio Orinoco, Venezuela), habitat (surface of small creeks, clear or blackwater), taxonomy (Characiformes, Lebiasinidae), diet (Ephemeroptera larvae, ants, insects, worms, crustaceans). FishBase TL of 4.2 cm reflects male; SeriouslyFish SL of 45–55 mm (TL ~5–6 cm) used as primary size figure, consistent with user brief of ~6 cm.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Splash Tetra (Copella arnoldi)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (6 cm), temperature (24–29°C), pH (6.0–7.2), hardness (up to 12 dH soft water), diet (mini-bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, daphnia, cyclops, flake, micro granules), breeding (5–10 eggs per leap, male splashes for 36 h, fry free-swimming after 2 days), grouping (6 minimum, 10+ preferred), tankmates (small Corydoras, dwarf cichlids, pencilfish, small blackwater tetras).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Splash Tetra (Copella arnoldi) Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (3–5 years), adult size (up to 3.5 in / ~8.9 cm for males; females ~2.75 in / ~7 cm — larger than SeriouslyFish SL figures, likely TL with fin extension included), temperature (73–79°F / 23–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), breeding behaviour (male leads female to leaf, pair leap together, male uses tail to fling water on eggs), minimum tank 15 gal / ~57 L for 6+ fish, peaceful temperament.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair leaps out of water together to deposit adhesive eggs on an overhanging leaf or surface 2–4 cm above the waterline. Male stands guard below and splashes water upward every ~30 min for 36–72 h until eggs hatch. Fry free-swimming after ~2 further days; accept infusoria then Artemia nauplii. Requires emergent spawning site, correct water level, and tight lid. Fairly straightforward once conditioned and correctly set up.
